question: Which classic pairing principle explains why a squeeze of lemon on fried fish works similarly to pairing Muscadet with oysters?
options: Tannin cuts through protein
Acid cuts through fat and richness
Sweetness balances salt
Alcohol enhances umami
explanation: Acidity in both lemon and high-acid wines like Muscadet cuts through the richness of fried or fatty foods, cleansing the palate between bites. This acid-fat interaction is one of the most fundamental pairing principles.
sources: The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil); WSET Level 3 Systematic Approach to Food and Wine Pairing

question: Which food does NOT follow the principle of 'what grows together goes together'?
options: Chianti with tomato-based pasta
Albariño with Galician pulpo
Sancerre with goat cheese (Crottin de Chavignol)
Barossa Shiraz with sushi
explanation: Chianti/tomato pasta (Tuscany), Albariño/pulpo (Galicia), and Sancerre/Crottin de Chavignol (Loire) are all classic regional pairings. Barossa Shiraz with sushi violates the regional principle since Barossa Valley is in South Australia, not Japan.
sources: World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson); The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: Why do tannic red wines pair well with grilled red meat?
options: Tannins enhance the smoky flavor of the grill
Proteins in meat bind with tannins, softening the wine's astringency
The alcohol in red wine breaks down fat molecules
Red meat increases the perception of fruit in the wine
explanation: Proteins and fats in red meat bind with tannin molecules, reducing the perception of astringency and making the wine taste smoother and more fruit-forward. This tannin-protein interaction is a cornerstone of classical pairing.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: A guest orders a plate of fresh asparagus with hollandaise sauce. Which wine would you recommend?
options: Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon
Grüner Veltliner from Kamptal
Vintage Port
Amarone della Valpolicella
explanation: Asparagus contains mercaptan compounds that clash with most wines, making it notoriously difficult to pair. Grüner Veltliner's herbal, white-pepper character and zippy acidity complement asparagus naturally. Austrian Grüner Veltliner is one of the few widely accepted pairings with asparagus.
sources: The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil); GuildSomm Pairing Database

question: What compound in artichokes makes wine taste sweeter, complicating pairing?
options: Capsaicin
Cynarin
Theobromine
Limonene
explanation: Cynarin, found in artichokes, temporarily inhibits sweet taste receptors. When the compound is washed away by a sip of water or wine, the receptors rebound and everything tastes sweeter. This effect can distort wine flavors significantly.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: Match each sauce type with its ideal wine pairing principle.

right: Off-dry aromatic to tame heat
Rich white with weight to match
High-acid red to match sauce acidity
Full-bodied red with tannin for protein-rich stock

explanation: Butter sauces need weight (rich Chardonnay), tomato sauces need acidity (Sangiovese), demi-glace needs tannin and body (Cabernet), and spicy coconut curries benefit from off-dry aromatics (Riesling Spätlese) whose residual sugar tempers heat.
sources: WSET Level 3 Study Guide; The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: When pairing wine with umami-rich foods like aged Parmesan or miso, what happens to the perception of tannic red wines?
options: Tannins appear softer and more integrated
Umami amplifies bitterness and astringency in the wine
Umami neutralizes the wine's acidity
There is no measurable interaction between umami and tannin
explanation: Umami intensifies the perception of bitterness and astringency in tannic wines, making them taste harsher. This is why high-umami dishes are better paired with fruity, low-tannin wines or wines with residual sugar rather than young, tannic reds.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); GuildSomm Advanced Theory

question: A table orders a cheese course featuring Roquefort, Comté, Époisses, and Crottin de Chavignol. They want one bottle for all four cheeses. What is your best recommendation?
options: Young Barolo
Sauternes
New Zealand Sauvignon Blanc
Beaujolais Nouveau
explanation: Sauternes is the classic pairing for Roquefort (sweet-salty contrast), and its richness and acidity also complement the nuttiness of Comté, the pungency of Époisses, and the tang of Crottin. When a single wine must serve a mixed cheese plate, sweet wines offer the most versatility.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); WSET Level 3 Study Guide

question: Which pairing does NOT rely on the principle of contrasting flavors?
options: Sauternes with Roquefort
Champagne with fried chicken
Off-dry Riesling with spicy Thai
Chianti with tomato-based pasta
explanation: Chianti with tomato pasta is a congruent (complementary) pairing: the high acidity in both the wine and the tomato sauce match. The others all rely on contrast: sweet vs. salty (Sauternes/Roquefort), acid/bubbles vs. fat (Champagne/fried chicken), and sugar vs. capsaicin heat (Riesling/Thai).
sources: The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil); WSET Level 3 Systematic Approach to Food and Wine Pairing

question: How does the cooking method of braising versus grilling affect wine pairing for the same cut of beef?
options: Braised beef requires higher alcohol; grilled beef requires lower alcohol
Braised beef's broken-down collagen favors acid-driven wines; grilled beef's charred crust favors tannic wines
Both preparations pair identically since the protein source is the same
Grilled beef requires sweeter wines due to Maillard compounds
explanation: Braising breaks down collagen into gelatin, creating a rich, unctuous texture best matched by wines with bright acidity (e.g., Barbera, Burgundy). Grilling produces a charred protein crust that binds with tannins effectively, favoring structured wines like Cabernet Sauvignon.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); GuildSomm Pairing Theory

question: Match each difficult-to-pair food with the best wine strategy.

right: Sparkling wine (bubbles cut richness of egg)
Brachetto d'Acqui or Banyuls (sweet red to match intensity)
Off-dry Gewürztraminer (sugar and aromatics tame numbing spice)
Rosé from Provence (acid and light fruit stand up to salt and umami)

explanation: Chocolate needs a wine at least as sweet as itself (Banyuls/Brachetto). Sichuan peppercorn's numbing heat is soothed by residual sugar and aromatics (Gewürztraminer). Eggs coat the palate, so bubbles provide textural contrast. Anchovies bring intense umami and salt, best balanced by fresh rosé.
sources: The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil); GuildSomm Pairing Database

question: A guest is having a 7-course kaiseki meal featuring raw fish, grilled eel, pickled vegetables, and miso soup. They request wine pairings for the entire meal. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Serve one bold red wine throughout to assert dominance over all courses
Progress from Champagne through Chablis, then Alsace Pinot Gris, incorporating off-dry wines for umami-heavy courses
Pair each course with a different full-bodied Napa Cabernet vintage
Serve only Sherry throughout because it is the most food-friendly wine
explanation: Kaiseki's sequential structure mirrors wine-pairing progression. Start with high-acid sparkling, move to lean whites for raw fish, richer whites for grilled items, and use off-dry wines to buffer umami in miso. This approach respects both the Japanese culinary philosophy and wine-pairing fundamentals.
sources: GuildSomm Advanced Pairing Seminars; WSET Level 3 Study Guide

question: In the context of wine and food pairing, what is the 'bridge ingredient' technique?
options: Using a shared aromatic compound in both the wine and a dish component to link them
Adding wine to the cooking sauce so the pairing is guaranteed
Serving bread between courses to reset the palate
Blending two wines to create a bridge between two courses
explanation: A bridge ingredient is a component added to a dish that shares aromatic or flavor compounds with the wine being served, creating a sensory link. For example, adding thyme to a lamb dish when serving Rhône Syrah, since both share herbal/garrigue notes from shared terpene compounds.
sources: GuildSomm Advanced Pairing Theory; Wine Science (Jamie Goode)

question: Why does Muscadet sur lie pair exceptionally well with raw oysters beyond just acidity?
options: The residual sugar in Muscadet buffers the brininess
Lees aging adds a subtle richness and savory quality that mirrors the oyster's minerality and umami
Muscadet's high tannin binds with the proteins in oyster flesh
The carbonic maceration process used for Muscadet creates effervescence that cleanses the palate
explanation: Muscadet sur lie gains autolytic character (amino acids, mannoproteins) from extended lees contact, giving it a subtle savory richness that bridges to the oyster's umami and mineral character. This goes beyond simple acidity to create a synergistic pairing.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); Wine Science (Jamie Goode)

question: A guest with advanced wine knowledge orders venison tartare with a quail egg yolk, truffle shavings, and a hazelnut vinaigrette. Which wine pairing demonstrates the deepest understanding of flavor interactions?
options: Vintage Champagne Blanc de Blancs — bubbles cut the egg richness, acid matches vinaigrette, autolytic notes echo truffle
Australian Shiraz — bold fruit stands up to game
Oregon Pinot Noir with some bottle age — earthy sous-bois notes mirror truffle, gentle tannins suit raw meat, acid handles vinaigrette
Paso Robles Zinfandel — high alcohol emphasizes the richness
explanation: Aged Oregon Pinot Noir offers earthy, sous-bois (forest floor) aromatics that create a bridge to truffle. Its moderate tannins won't overwhelm raw meat (unlike cooked), its acidity balances the hazelnut vinaigrette, and its medium body respects the dish's delicacy. This is a multi-dimensional pairing.
sources: GuildSomm Advanced Pairing Theory; The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: Which volatile compound shared by both Gewürztraminer and lychee-glazed duck makes the pairing work on a molecular level?
options: Methoxypyrazine
Cis-rose oxide
Rotundone
4-MMP (4-mercapto-4-methylpentan-2-one)
explanation: Cis-rose oxide is the monoterpene responsible for the floral, lychee-like aroma in both Gewürztraminer grapes and lychee fruit. When lychee appears as a glaze component, it creates a direct aromatic bridge to Gewürztraminer at the molecular level.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: Which pairing does NOT demonstrate a scientifically validated synergistic interaction?
options: Champagne with caviar (effervescence cleanses fat, autolysis mirrors fishiness)
Sauternes with foie gras (sweetness cuts richness, botrytis adds complementary complexity)
Aged Barolo with white truffle pasta (nebbiolo's rose/tar aromatics complement truffle's volatile sulfur compounds)
Napa Cabernet with milk chocolate mousse (tannin complements cocoa butter)
explanation: Milk chocolate's high sugar content clashes with dry Cabernet's tannins, which amplify bitterness in the presence of sugar. The other three are well-documented synergistic pairings where specific chemical interactions produce enhanced flavor experiences.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); GuildSomm Advanced Sensory Science

question: When capsaicin from chili peppers binds to TRPV1 receptors, how does ethanol in wine interact with this process?
options: Ethanol blocks TRPV1 receptors, reducing the perception of heat
Ethanol is a TRPV1 agonist that lowers the receptor's activation threshold, intensifying the burning sensation
Ethanol converts capsaicin into a non-irritating compound
Ethanol has no interaction with TRPV1 receptors
explanation: Ethanol is a known TRPV1 agonist. It lowers the activation temperature of TRPV1 receptors from 43°C to approximately 34°C (below body temperature), which is why high-alcohol wines intensify the burn of spicy food. This is why lower-alcohol, off-dry wines work better with chili-based dishes.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); Journal of Neuroscience research on TRPV1 and ethanol

question: You are building a wine pairing for a dish of A5 Wagyu beef tataki with ponzu, daikon, and shiso. The wine list includes: (A) 2015 Burgundy Premier Cru, (B) 2018 Barossa Shiraz, (C) 2010 Champagne Vintage Brut, (D) 2016 Willamette Valley Pinot Noir. Which pairing best addresses ALL components?
options: 2015 Burgundy Premier Cru — its tertiary complexity matches the dish's layered flavors, acidity addresses ponzu, Burgundian earthiness complements shiso
2018 Barossa Shiraz — the richness matches Wagyu's marbling
2010 Champagne Vintage Brut — bubbles and age cut through Wagyu fat, acid mirrors ponzu citrus, toasty autolysis bridges to seared exterior
2016 Willamette Valley Pinot Noir — light enough for raw beef preparation
explanation: The aged vintage Champagne addresses every element: its persistent mousse and acid cut through A5 Wagyu's extreme marbling, the citrus notes mirror ponzu, the autolytic complexity (brioche, toast) bridges to the seared exterior of tataki, and its palate-cleansing ability resets between the rich beef and sharp daikon/shiso. This is a holistic, multi-dimensional pairing.
sources: GuildSomm CMS Theory Exam Preparation; Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: In molecular gastronomy pairing theory, the 'food-bridge hypothesis' proposes that successful pairings share key volatile compounds. Which analytical technique is primarily used to identify these shared volatiles?
options: Brix refractometry
Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS)
Spectrophotometric titration
Nephelometric turbidity measurement
explanation: GC-MS is the primary analytical tool for identifying volatile aromatic compounds in both wine and food. Researchers like François Benzi and others at INRA have used GC-MS to map shared volatiles between foods and wines, supporting the molecular pairing hypothesis that shared key odorants predict pairing success.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); International Journal of Gastronomy and Food Science

question: Which is the most important rule when pairing wine with dessert?
options: The wine should always be red
The wine should be at least as sweet as the dessert
The wine should be as dry as possible
The wine should be served warm
explanation: If the dessert is sweeter than the wine, the wine will taste thin, bitter, and sour by comparison. The wine must match or exceed the sweetness level of the dish to maintain balance.
sources: WSET Level 2 Study Guide; The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: A guest orders a Caesar salad as their starter. Which wine should you suggest?
options: Barolo
Verdicchio dei Castelli di Jesi
Vintage Port
Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon
explanation: Caesar salad features anchovy (umami), Parmesan (umami), lemon (acid), and lettuce (bitter). Verdicchio's bright acidity, saline mineral quality, and light body complement all these elements without clashing. Heavy tannic wines would taste harsh against the umami and vinaigrette.
sources: GuildSomm Pairing Database; The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: Which cheese category generally pairs best with tannic red wines?
options: Fresh cheeses like mozzarella
Hard aged cheeses like aged Cheddar or Manchego
Blue cheeses like Stilton
Bloomy rind cheeses like Brie
explanation: Hard aged cheeses have concentrated protein and fat content that binds with and softens tannins. Their lower moisture content and nutty, caramelized flavors complement the structural components of tannic reds. Blue cheeses clash with tannins due to high umami, and fresh/bloomy cheeses lack the intensity.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); WSET Level 3 Study Guide

question: Why does Champagne pair so well with potato chips?
options: The sugar in Champagne balances the salt
The high acidity and effervescence cut through fat and salt while the yeast autolysis notes complement fried starch
Champagne's tannins bind with the potato proteins
The cool serving temperature numbs the palate to the salt
explanation: Champagne's high acidity and CO2 effervescence physically scrub fat from the palate, while the salt on chips enhances the wine's fruit perception. Additionally, autolytic notes (toasty, brioche-like flavors from lees aging) bridge to the fried, starchy flavors of the chips.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); GuildSomm Pairing Theory

question: Match each regional cheese with its classic regional wine partner.

right: Lambrusco
Sauternes
Chablis or Meursault
Tempranillo Reserva

explanation: Roquefort with Sauternes is a legendary sweet-salty contrast pairing from neighboring French regions. Époisses from Burgundy pairs with Burgundian whites. Manchego and Tempranillo are both La Mancha staples. Parmigiano-Reggiano and Lambrusco are an Emilian tradition — the sparkling wine's acidity cuts the cheese's umami intensity.
sources: World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson); Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: Which pairing strategy is NOT appropriate for a dish with high chili heat?
options: Choose a wine with residual sugar
Choose a wine with low alcohol
Choose a wine with high tannin and high alcohol
Choose a wine with aromatic intensity to compete with spice aromatics
explanation: High-tannin, high-alcohol wines are the worst choice for chili-spiced foods. Alcohol intensifies the burn of capsaicin (via TRPV1 receptor activation), and tannins add astringent bitterness that compounds the irritation. Residual sugar, low alcohol, and aromatic intensity all help manage heat.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); WSET Level 3 Study Guide

question: A guest requests a wine pairing for bone marrow on toast with parsley and caper salad. Which wine best addresses both the rich marrow and the bright condiment?
options: Cru Beaujolais (Morgon) — fruity enough for marrow, acid for capers
South African Chenin Blanc — tropical fruit overwhelms the dish
Châteauneuf-du-Pape — its earthy richness and moderate acid bridges marrow and herbal salad, with enough structure for the fat
German Riesling Kabinett — too light for the richness of marrow
explanation: Châteauneuf-du-Pape's combination of earthy garrigue (herbs, lavender) notes echoes the parsley/caper garnish, while its full body and moderate tannins stand up to the extreme richness of bone marrow. The wine's savory, herb-inflected profile creates a bridge between the two contrasting elements on the plate.
sources: GuildSomm Advanced Pairing Theory; The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: What is the role of salivary proline-rich proteins (PRPs) in the perception of food and wine pairings involving tannins?
options: PRPs enhance tannin perception, making wines taste more astringent with food
PRPs bind and precipitate tannins, causing the drying sensation of astringency; fatty foods compete for this binding, reducing perceived astringency
PRPs convert tannins into flavor-active compounds
PRPs have no role in tannin perception
explanation: Salivary PRPs normally lubricate the mouth. Tannins bind to and precipitate these proteins, causing the dry, astringent sensation. When fatty, protein-rich foods are consumed simultaneously, food proteins compete with salivary PRPs for tannin binding, leaving more PRPs available for lubrication and reducing perceived astringency.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: Regarding the Maillard reaction and wine pairing, which statement is most accurate?
options: Maillard products in grilled foods have no impact on wine pairing decisions
Maillard reaction products (pyrazines, furanones, thiazoles) in seared foods create aromatic bridges to barrel-aged wines that contain similar compounds from oak toasting
The Maillard reaction only occurs in food and never in wine production
Maillard products make all foods pair exclusively with white wines
explanation: The Maillard reaction in seared/grilled foods produces heterocyclic compounds (pyrazines, furanones) that overlap with compounds generated during oak barrel toasting. This shared chemistry creates aromatic bridges between grilled dishes and barrel-aged wines, explaining why grilled steak pairs so well with oaked Cabernet.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); GuildSomm Advanced Sensory Science

question: Which compound is NOT relevant to understanding why certain wines pair well with mushroom-based dishes?
options: 1-Octen-3-ol (mushroom alcohol, found in both Pinot Noir and mushrooms)
Sotolone (found in oxidized wines and some fungi)
Methoxypyrazines (green bell pepper note from unripe grapes)
Dimethyl sulfide at low levels (earthy, truffle-like, found in aged wines)
explanation: Methoxypyrazines produce green, vegetal notes (bell pepper, grass) associated with unripe Cabernet Sauvignon family grapes — not mushroom aromatics. The other three compounds all have mushroom/earthy/truffle aromatic qualities that create genuine molecular bridges between wine and mushroom dishes.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: You are pairing wines for a multi-course tasting featuring sea urchin with yuzu, followed by squab with cherry jus, then aged Gruyère with honeycomb. The sommelier team proposes: Course 1: Fino Sherry, Course 2: Cornas (Northern Rhône Syrah), Course 3: Jurançon moelleux. What is the flaw in this progression?
options: The wines are too expensive for the menu price point
The progression is flawless and demonstrates ideal pairing logic
Fino Sherry is too oxidative for delicate sea urchin — a young, reductive Chablis or Muscadet would better serve the course
The jump from Fino Sherry's salinity to Cornas's power is too abrupt — an intermediate white or light red course should bridge them
explanation: While each individual pairing has merit, the progression fails in its lack of a transition between the light, saline Fino and the powerful, tannic Cornas. A well-designed wine progression should have no jarring jumps. An intermediate wine (e.g., aged white Burgundy or lighter Rhône red) would create a smoother arc.
sources: GuildSomm CMS Theory Exam Preparation; Court of Master Sommeliers Study Materials

question: Research on trigeminal interactions shows that carbonation in sparkling wine activates which receptor, and how does this affect pairing with fatty foods?
options: TRPA1 receptors — carbonation creates a cooling sensation that numbs fat perception

Carbonic anhydrase converts CO2 to carbonic acid on the tongue, stimulating acid-sensing ion channels (ASICs) and mechanoreceptors, physically and chemically cleansing fatty coatings from oral surfaces
TRPM8 receptors — the same pathway as menthol, creating a fresh sensation
Piezo2 receptors only — purely a mechanical scrubbing action with no chemical component
explanation: CO2 in sparkling wine is converted to carbonic acid by carbonic anhydrase enzymes on the tongue, stimulating both chemical (ASICs) and mechanical receptors. This dual action physically disrupts lipid films on oral surfaces and chemically stimulates saliva production, making sparkling wine exceptionally effective at cutting through fatty foods.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); Journal of Sensory Studies on oral trigeminal responses

question: In studies of retronasal olfaction during food-wine interaction, what phenomenon explains why a pairing can create entirely new aromas not present in either the food or wine alone?
options: Olfactory fatigue causes the brain to invent new smells

Perceptual blending and suppression — when volatiles from food and wine merge in the retronasal cavity, the brain can synthesize novel percepts through configural processing, perceiving the mixture as a new odor object rather than its components
The tongue's taste receptors can detect aromatic compounds when mixed with saliva
New chemical compounds are literally created through enzymatic reactions in the mouth
explanation: Retronasal olfactory research shows that when food and wine volatiles co-arrive at olfactory receptors, configural processing can create emergent odor percepts — new aromas that don't exist in either component alone. This phenomenon, studied by researchers like Gordon Shepherd, explains why great pairings produce a 'third flavor' experience.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); Neurogastronomy (Gordon Shepherd)

question: Which wine style generally pairs best with spicy Thai green curry?
options: Dry, tannic Cabernet Sauvignon
Off-dry Riesling
Full-bodied oaked Chardonnay
Dry rosé from Provence
explanation: Off-dry Riesling's residual sugar helps tame the chili heat, its low alcohol avoids intensifying the burn, and its bright acidity provides a refreshing counterpoint to the rich coconut milk base of Thai green curry.
sources: The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil); WSET Level 2 Study Guide

question: Which interaction is NOT a documented mechanism by which food modifies wine perception?
options: Fat reducing perceived astringency through competitive protein binding
Salt suppressing bitterness and enhancing fruit perception
Umami amplifying perceived bitterness and astringency in tannic wines
Capsaicin directly denaturing anthocyanin pigments in red wine, reducing color intensity
explanation: Capsaicin does not denature anthocyanins. Its interaction with wine is purely perceptual (via TRPV1 receptor agonism and trigeminal nerve stimulation). The other three are well-documented mechanisms: fat-tannin competition for salivary proteins, salt's suppression of bitter taste, and umami's amplification of astringency.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); GuildSomm Advanced Sensory Science

question: A Master Sommelier candidate must pair a single wine with a dish of seared diver scallop, cauliflower purée, brown butter, capers, raisins, and pine nuts. Considering textural weight, acid balance, aromatic bridges, and sweet-savory interplay, which wine shows the deepest understanding?
options: NV Krug Grande Cuvée — weight and autolytic complexity for scallop, acid for brown butter, oxidative notes for capers
Mature Savennières (Chenin Blanc) — waxy texture mirrors brown butter, quince/honey notes bridge to raisins, high acid cuts richness, saline mineral finish echoes capers
Meursault Premier Cru — weight matches the dish but lacks aromatic bridges to raisins and capers
Condrieu (Viognier) — stone fruit aromatics for the raisins but too low in acid for the brown butter richness
explanation: Mature Savennières addresses every component: its waxy, lanolin texture parallels brown butter; its aged quince/honey aromatics bridge to golden raisins; its piercing acid handles the richness; its flinty, saline minerality echoes capers; and its medium-plus body matches the scallop's weight. This demonstrates the Master Sommelier-level ability to find a single wine that creates multiple bridges across a complex dish.
sources: Court of Master Sommeliers Study Materials; GuildSomm CMS Theory Exam Preparation

question: Match each volatile compound bridge to the correct food-wine pairing it explains.

right: Malolactic Chardonnay with lobster in butter sauce
Northern Rhône Syrah with black pepper-crusted venison
Sancerre with passion fruit pavlova
Muscat with elderflower and lychee dessert

explanation: Rotundone is the sesquiterpene responsible for black pepper aroma in both Syrah and peppercorns. 4-MMP/3-MH are polyfunctional thiols giving tropical (passion fruit) character to Sauvignon Blanc. Linalool is a monoterpene shared by Muscat, elderflower, and lychee. Diacetyl is produced during malolactic fermentation and mirrors the butter in the dish.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: A guest wants to pair wine with a Thanksgiving meal featuring roast turkey, cranberry sauce, sweet potato casserole, and green bean casserole. Which single bottle addresses the broadest range of these dishes?
options: Alsace Pinot Gris Vendange Tardive — too sweet for savory dishes
Willamette Valley Pinot Noir — red fruit echoes cranberry, moderate tannin suits turkey protein, earth complements root vegetables, acid cuts through casserole richness
Argentine Malbec — too tannic for turkey breast
Mosel Riesling Spätlese — acid and sweetness help but lacks body for the meal's overall weight
explanation: Oregon Pinot Noir is a Thanksgiving workhorse: its red fruit aromatics bridge to cranberry sauce, moderate tannins complement turkey without overwhelming white meat, its earthiness echoes root vegetables, and its bright acidity handles the richness of casseroles. Its medium body matches the meal's moderate weight.
sources: The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil); GuildSomm Pairing Database

question: What is the simplest guideline for matching wine weight to food weight?
options: Always serve red wine with every dish
Match light wines with light dishes and full-bodied wines with rich dishes
Always serve the most expensive wine available
Pair only by color: white wine with white food, red wine with red food
explanation: The most fundamental pairing principle is matching the weight (body) of the wine to the weight of the dish. A delicate Pinot Grigio would be overwhelmed by a beef stew, just as a powerful Barolo would overpower a simple green salad.
sources: WSET Level 2 Study Guide; The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: In the context of multi-sensory perception, how does the visual presentation of a dish influence wine pairing perception according to crossmodal correspondence research?
options: Visual presentation has no effect on flavor perception

Round plate shapes and warm-colored plating enhance perception of sweetness and fruitiness in paired wines, while angular presentations enhance perceived acidity and bitterness, affecting pairing satisfaction through crossmodal correspondences
Only the color of the wine glass matters
Visual cues only affect aroma, not taste perception
explanation: Crossmodal research by Charles Spence and others demonstrates that visual cues (plate shape, color, presentation geometry) systematically influence taste and flavor perception. Round shapes and warm colors prime sweetness/fruitiness, while angular shapes and cool colors prime acidity/bitterness. Sommeliers at the highest level consider these presentation factors.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); Gastrophysics (Charles Spence)

question: What devastating vine pest arrived in Europe in the 1860s, destroying most of the continent's vineyards?
options: Oidium (powdery mildew)
Phylloxera
Downy mildew
Pierce's disease
explanation: Phylloxera (Dactylosphaera vitifoliae), a root-feeding aphid native to eastern North America, was accidentally introduced to Europe around 1863. It devastated European vineyards because Vitis vinifera had no natural resistance, ultimately destroying roughly two-thirds of European vineyards.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: Which event in 1976 shocked the wine world by having California wines beat French wines in a blind tasting?
options: The Berlin Tasting
The Judgment of Paris
The Spurrier Challenge
The Napa Showdown
explanation: The Judgment of Paris, organized by British wine merchant Steven Spurrier on May 24, 1976, saw a Napa Valley Chardonnay (Chateau Montelena 1973) and Cabernet Sauvignon (Stag's Leap 1973) defeat top Burgundy and Bordeaux wines in a blind tasting judged by French experts.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: Which of these was NOT a first-growth (Premier Cru) in the original 1855 Bordeaux Classification?
options: Château Lafite Rothschild
Château Latour
Château Pétrus
Château Margaux
explanation: The 1855 Classification covered Médoc (plus Haut-Brion from Graves). Château Pétrus is in Pomerol, which was never part of the 1855 Classification. The four original first growths were Lafite, Latour, Margaux, and Haut-Brion. Mouton Rothschild was elevated to first-growth status in 1973.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: Which country is considered the birthplace of winemaking, with evidence of viticulture dating back approximately 8,000 years?
options: Greece
Italy
Georgia
France
explanation: Archaeological evidence from the South Caucasus region of Georgia, including residue analysis from clay vessels (qvevri) at the Gadachrili Gora site, dates winemaking to approximately 6000 BCE, making Georgia the oldest known site of deliberate wine production.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); PNAS (Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences) 2017 study on early Georgian winemaking

question: A guest asks whether Dom Pérignon really 'invented' Champagne. What is the historically accurate response?
options: Yes, he invented the méthode champenoise in 1693
No — sparkling wine in Champagne developed gradually; Dom Pérignon actually worked to PREVENT bubbles but contributed advances in blending, pressing, and quality
He invented Champagne but the English discovered it independently at the same time
He only invented the wire cage (muselet) for the cork
explanation: The Dom Pérignon myth is widely debunked. As cellar master at Hautvillers Abbey (1668-1715), he actually tried to eliminate bubbles, which were considered a fault. His real contributions were pioneering blending of different vineyard parcels, improving pressing techniques for white juice from red grapes, and advancing cork usage. English scientist Christopher Merret documented adding sugar for secondary fermentation in 1662, before Pérignon's tenure.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: Match each wine law milestone with its country and approximate year.

right: Spain, 1932
France, 1935
Italy, 1963
United States, 1980

explanation: France's AOC (Appellation d'Origine Contrôlée) was established in 1935 under INAO. Italy's DOC (Denominazione di Origine Controllata) followed in 1963. The US AVA (American Viticultural Area) system began in 1980. Spain's DO system was actually established in 1932, making it technically the earliest of these four.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: What was the primary solution that saved European viticulture from phylloxera?
options: Chemical fumigation of all vineyards
Grafting European Vitis vinifera vines onto resistant American rootstocks
Developing genetically modified vinifera vines
Flooding vineyards permanently to drown the aphid
explanation: The eventual solution was grafting Vitis vinifera (European wine grape) scions onto rootstocks of American vine species (V. riparia, V. rupestris, V. berlandieri), which had evolved natural resistance to phylloxera. This practice continues today and is standard in most wine regions worldwide.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); Wine Science (Jamie Goode)

question: What role did Louis Pasteur play in the history of wine?
options: He invented the wine press
He discovered that fermentation was caused by living microorganisms (yeast), not spontaneous chemical reactions, and developed pasteurization to prevent wine spoilage
He created the first Bordeaux classification
He was the first to bring Vitis vinifera to the Americas
explanation: In the 1850s-60s, Louis Pasteur demonstrated that fermentation was a biological process driven by yeast, not merely a chemical reaction. His work in Études sur le vin (1866) also identified bacteria as the cause of wine spoilage, leading to pasteurization. This revolutionized winemaking science.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); Wine Science (Jamie Goode)

question: Which figure was NOT directly instrumental in the development of California's modern wine industry?
options: André Tchelistcheff
Robert Mondavi
Baron Philippe de Rothschild
Agoston Haraszthy
explanation: Baron Philippe de Rothschild was a pivotal figure in Bordeaux (Mouton Rothschild), not California. André Tchelistcheff revolutionized Napa winemaking at Beaulieu Vineyard from 1938. Robert Mondavi founded his iconic winery in 1966. Agoston Haraszthy is considered the father of California viticulture, importing European varieties in the 1850s-60s.
sources: The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil); Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: US Prohibition (1920-1933) devastated the American wine industry, but which legal loophole allowed some winemaking to continue?
options: Wines under 5% ABV were exempt
The Volstead Act permitted production of up to 200 gallons per household per year for personal use of 'non-intoxicating fruit juice'
All religious wine was banned without exception
Only imported wine was legal during Prohibition
explanation: Section 29 of the Volstead Act allowed heads of household to produce up to 200 gallons of 'non-intoxicating cider and fruit juices' per year. This loophole was exploited for home winemaking, and grape shipments to home winemakers actually increased during Prohibition. Sacramental wine production was also permitted for religious use.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: Match each ancient civilization with its primary contribution to wine history.

right: Systematic vineyard classification and terroir mapping (e.g., Burgundy crus)
Earliest known deliberate winemaking in qvevri
Spread vine cuttings across the Mediterranean trade routes
Large-scale viticulture, amphorae trade, and documented varietal preferences

explanation: Georgians pioneered qvevri winemaking (~6000 BCE). Phoenician traders spread viticulture across the Mediterranean. Romans industrialized wine production and documented grape varieties and vineyard practices. Cistercian and Benedictine monks meticulously mapped terroir in Burgundy, creating the cru classification system.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: A guest asks why Château Mouton Rothschild was not originally classified as a First Growth in 1855. What is the correct historical explanation?
options: The owner refused to participate in the classification
Mouton was a Second Growth in 1855; after decades of lobbying by Baron Philippe de Rothschild, it was elevated to First Growth in 1973 — the only change ever made to the original classification
Mouton did not exist in 1855
Mouton was in Graves, which was excluded from the 1855 classification
explanation: Mouton was classified as the top of the Second Growths in 1855. Baron Philippe de Rothschild famously campaigned for decades for promotion, adopting the motto 'Premier ne puis, second ne daigne, Mouton suis' (First I cannot be, second I do not deign to be, Mouton I am). In 1973, it was officially elevated to Premier Cru — the only reclassification in the system's history.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: Which specific wines won in each category at the 1976 Judgment of Paris?
options: Ridge Monte Bello (red) and Freemark Abbey (white)
Stag's Leap Wine Cellars 1973 S.L.V. Cabernet Sauvignon (red) and Chateau Montelena 1973 Chardonnay (white)
Opus One (red) and Robert Mondavi Fumé Blanc (white)
Heitz Martha's Vineyard (red) and Stony Hill Chardonnay (white)
explanation: The 1973 Stag's Leap Wine Cellars S.L.V. Cabernet Sauvignon beat top Bordeaux including Mouton Rothschild and Haut-Brion. The 1973 Chateau Montelena Chardonnay defeated prestigious Burgundies including Meursault-Charmes and Puligny-Montrachet. Both results stunned the French judges and international wine press.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: What was André Tchelistcheff's most significant technical contribution to Napa Valley winemaking?
options: He introduced mechanical harvesting

He championed cold fermentation for whites, malolactic fermentation for reds, and was a pioneer of frost protection — transforming Napa winemaking from rustic to refined during his tenure at Beaulieu Vineyard
He invented concrete egg fermenters
He planted the first Cabernet Sauvignon vines in Napa
explanation: André Tchelistcheff, a Russian-born enologist, arrived at Beaulieu Vineyard in 1938 and became the most influential winemaker in California history. He introduced temperature-controlled fermentation, deliberately employed malolactic fermentation in reds, pioneered frost protection with wind machines, and mentored an entire generation of California winemakers.
sources: The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil); Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: Which event did NOT directly result from the phylloxera crisis?
options: Widespread adoption of grafted rootstocks in European viticulture
The near-total replanting of French vineyards between 1880 and 1910
The creation of the AOC system in 1935
The introduction of Cabernet Sauvignon to Australia
explanation: Cabernet Sauvignon was brought to Australia by James Busby in the 1830s, decades before phylloxera hit Europe. The other three are direct consequences: grafting became standard, French vineyards were replanted, and the economic devastation and subsequent fraud (cheap wine sold as famous appellations) eventually led to the creation of the AOC system to protect regional authenticity.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: What was the significance of the 1976 Judgment of Paris for the global wine industry beyond just California?
options: It only affected California wine prices

It demonstrated that quality wine could be produced outside traditional European regions, catalyzing the global expansion of fine wine production in the New World and undermining the assumption of inherent European superiority
It led to the immediate collapse of the Bordeaux market
It proved that blind tasting is unreliable
explanation: The Judgment of Paris did not just boost California — it shattered the paradigm that only Europe could produce world-class wine. It empowered winemakers in Australia, Chile, South Africa, New Zealand, and elsewhere to pursue quality, fundamentally reshaping the global wine industry and ushering in the modern era of New World fine wine.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: A collector shows you a bottle labeled 'Château Lafite 1870.' They claim it was pre-phylloxera and therefore grown on ungrafted vines. Is this plausible?
options: No — phylloxera destroyed all French vines before 1870

Yes — phylloxera was first detected in France around 1863 in the southern Rhône, but it did not reach Bordeaux's Médoc significantly until the mid-1870s, so an 1870 Lafite could indeed be from ungrafted, pre-phylloxera vines
No — all French wines were grafted by 1865
Yes — but only if it was from sandy soil, which resists phylloxera
explanation: Phylloxera was first identified in France near Roquemaure in the southern Rhône around 1863. It spread gradually and did not devastate the Médoc until the late 1870s-1880s. An 1870 Lafite would indeed be from ungrafted Vitis vinifera on its own roots — a true pre-phylloxera wine. The wine's authentication would still require provenance verification.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: Which ancient Roman author wrote the most comprehensive treatise on viticulture and wine, including descriptions of grape varieties, vineyard management, and winemaking techniques?
options: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia)
Julius Caesar (De Bello Gallico)
Columella (De Re Rustica)
Virgil (Georgics)
explanation: Lucius Junius Moderatus Columella's De Re Rustica (c. 65 CE) is the most detailed ancient agricultural manual, with Book III and IV devoted entirely to viticulture. While Pliny's Natural History also discusses wine extensively, Columella provides the most practical and comprehensive viticultural guidance from antiquity.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: Match each historical figure to their key contribution to wine history.

right: Developed Bordeaux mixture (copper sulfate) to combat downy mildew
Published first documented description of adding sugar for deliberate sparkling wine production (1662)
Systematized vine training methods still used today (Guyot system)
Modernized Paris, indirectly affecting Bordeaux wine trade through railway development

explanation: Jules Guyot developed the vine training system bearing his name. Haussmann's urban modernization and railway expansion improved Bordeaux's access to Parisian markets. Christopher Merret presented a paper to the Royal Society in 1662 describing how sugar addition created bubbles — predating Dom Pérignon. Millardet discovered Bordeaux mixture (CuSO4 + lime) in 1885 to fight downy mildew.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); Wine Science (Jamie Goode)

question: What was the historical significance of the Winzergenossenschaft (cooperative winery) movement in Germany, and when did it originate?
options: It was a 20th-century marketing strategy with no historical roots

The first German wine cooperative was founded in 1868 in Mayschoß (Ahr), inspired by Friedrich Wilhelm Raiffeisen's cooperative principles, and became a model for cooperative winemaking worldwide, democratizing quality wine production for small growers
Cooperatives were banned in Germany until 1950
The movement began in Burgundy and was imported to Germany in 1920
explanation: The Mayschoß-Altenahr cooperative, founded in 1868 in the Ahr Valley, is the oldest wine cooperative in the world. Inspired by Raiffeisen's cooperative banking principles, it allowed small growers to pool resources for winemaking, storage, and marketing. This model spread globally and remains vital in many wine regions today.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); German Wine Institute (DWI) historical records

question: Which event did NOT occur in 1855, the year of the famous Bordeaux Classification?
options: Napoleon III ordered a classification of Bordeaux wines for the Paris Exposition Universelle
The Classification of the wines of the Médoc was drawn up by the Bordeaux wine brokers (courtiers)
The Classification of Sauternes and Barsac was established alongside the Médoc classification
The first Grand Cru Classé of Saint-Émilion was officially established
explanation: The Saint-Émilion classification was not established until 1955, a full century after the 1855 Bordeaux Classification. In 1855, the Médoc classification (plus Haut-Brion from Graves) and the Sauternes/Barsac classification were both created for Napoleon III's Paris Exposition. The Saint-Émilion system is notably different in that it is revised approximately every 10 years.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: A student asks about the historical relationship between the English and the development of specific wine regions. Which answer is most complete?
options: England only influenced Champagne

English demand and trade relationships directly shaped Bordeaux (claret trade from the 12th century via Eleanor of Aquitaine's marriage), Port (Methuen Treaty 1703 drove English merchants to the Douro), Sherry (English merchants established bodegas in Jerez), and Madeira (English trade route stopover)
The English had no significant influence on wine production outside England
English influence was limited to inventing the glass bottle
explanation: English influence on wine is vast: Bordeaux's claret trade dates to 1152 when Eleanor of Aquitaine married Henry II. The Methuen Treaty (1703) gave Portuguese wines preferential tariffs, driving English merchants to fortify Douro wines (creating Port). English merchants dominated Jerez's sherry trade. Madeira evolved as a wine for English ships on Atlantic voyages.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: What was the legal and commercial significance of the 1911 Champagne riots?
options: They were protests against cork prices

Vignerons in the Aube revolted because the 1908 delimitation of Champagne excluded their vineyards, and Marne growers rioted in 1911 over fraud (northern French and foreign grapes being sold as Champagne); these events directly accelerated the establishment of France's appellation laws
They were celebrations of a particularly good harvest
They were labor disputes unrelated to wine production
explanation: The Champagne riots of 1911 were triggered by economic distress: the 1908 delimitation excluded the Aube, and widespread fraud (cheap grapes relabeled as Champagne) devastated honest growers. Marne vignerons destroyed négociant cellars in Aÿ and Épernay. These events were critical precursors to France's appellation laws, ultimately leading to the AOC system created in 1935.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: What critical role did the Cistercian monks play in the development of Burgundy's vineyard classification, and what was their methodology?
options: They simply inherited Roman vineyard maps

The Cistercians at Clos de Vougeot and elsewhere systematically observed differences in soil, drainage, exposure, and microclimate across plots over centuries, tasting wines from individual parcels separately and documenting quality differences — essentially inventing the concept of terroir-based classification that became Burgundy's cru system
They classified vineyards solely by altitude
Their only contribution was building stone walls around vineyards
explanation: Cistercian monks, particularly at Clos de Vougeot (founded 1098), were meticulous empiricists. They vinified parcels separately, observed soil and exposure differences, and over centuries mapped the quality hierarchy of individual vineyard plots. Their legacy is the foundation of Burgundy's lieu-dit and cru system — the world's most elaborate expression of terroir classification.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: Which statement about the history of phylloxera is NOT accurate?
options: Some European vineyards on sandy soils (e.g., Colares in Portugal, parts of the Camargue) survived phylloxera because the pest cannot move through sand
Chile remains largely phylloxera-free due to its geographic isolation (Andes, Pacific, Atacama Desert)
Phylloxera was definitively eradicated from all European vineyards by 1920, and grafting is no longer necessary
Ungrafted pre-phylloxera vines still exist in some regions, including parts of Australia (Barossa Valley old-vine Shiraz) and Chile
explanation: Phylloxera was never eradicated from Europe — it remains present in the soil. Grafting onto resistant American rootstocks is still essential and practiced universally in Europe. The sandy soil survival (Colares, Camargue), Chile's freedom from phylloxera, and surviving ungrafted old vines in Barossa and Chile are all accurate historical facts.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); Wine Science (Jamie Goode)

question: In the context of wine authentication, what technique was used to detect fraud in supposedly pre-1945 wines by measuring the presence of cesium-137?
options: Mass spectrometry of cork residue

Measuring cesium-137 and strontium-90 in the wine, which are nuclear fission products that did not exist in the atmosphere before the first nuclear detonations in 1945; their absence confirms pre-nuclear-age origin
Carbon-14 dating of the label paper
Spectrophotometric analysis of anthocyanin degradation
explanation: Cesium-137 (137Cs) is a radioactive isotope produced exclusively by nuclear fission. It was absent from the environment before the Trinity test in July 1945. Wines made before this date contain no detectable 137Cs, while post-1945 wines contain trace amounts. This technique has been used to authenticate (or debunk) wines claiming pre-war vintages, notably in the Rudy Kurniawan fraud case.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: Which country produces the most wine by volume in the world?
options: France
United States
Italy
Spain
explanation: Italy and France have alternated as the world's largest wine producers by volume for decades. Italy has led in most recent years, typically producing 45-55 million hectoliters annually, followed closely by France and then Spain.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); OIV (International Organisation of Vine and Wine) statistics

question: Which of these is NOT one of the original four First Growths from the 1855 Bordeaux Classification?
options: Château Haut-Brion
Château Lafite Rothschild
Château Mouton Rothschild
Château Latour
explanation: The original 1855 Classification named four First Growths: Lafite, Latour, Margaux, and Haut-Brion. Mouton Rothschild was classified as a Second Growth and was not elevated to First Growth status until 1973.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: A student claims that the ancient Greeks fermented wine in large clay amphorae and added seawater and pine resin to preserve it. Is this historically accurate?
options: No — Greeks only drank beer
Yes — ancient Greeks used pitched amphorae and commonly added seawater and pine resin to preserve wine; retsina (pine-resinated wine) survives today as a direct descendant of this practice
They added resin but never seawater
This describes Roman, not Greek, practice
explanation: Ancient Greeks commonly sealed amphorae with pine resin and added both resin and seawater to wine as preservatives. This practice is well documented by ancient authors. Modern Greek retsina is the direct descendant of this ancient technique, using resin from Aleppo pines during fermentation.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: Why was the Methuen Treaty of 1703 significant for the development of Port wine?
options: It banned French wines from Portugal

It gave Portuguese wines preferential import tariffs into England over French wines, driving English merchants to the Douro Valley and incentivizing the development of fortified Port to survive the sea voyage
It established the first Port wine classification
It created the Douro demarcation
explanation: The Methuen Treaty established preferential tariffs for Portuguese wines entering England (lower duty than French wines), driven by political tensions with France. English merchants flocked to the Douro, and fortification with grape spirit developed to stabilize wines for the long sea journey to England. The Douro demarcation came later in 1756 under the Marquis de Pombal.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: Match each wine fraud case with its key detail.

right: Alleged Thomas Jefferson ownership; questioned by Bill Koch; never authenticated by forensic analysis
Convicted in 2013 for counterfeiting rare Burgundy and Bordeaux worth millions using blends and fake labels
Italian investigation revealing unauthorized blending of non-Sangiovese grapes in Brunello di Montalcino
New Jersey blind tasting event sometimes compared to 1976 Paris, not a fraud case

explanation: Rudy Kurniawan was the most notorious modern wine counterfeiter, convicted in 2013. The Jefferson Bottles were allegedly owned by Thomas Jefferson but were exposed as likely fakes. Brunellopoli uncovered producers adulterating Brunello with non-permitted grape varieties. The Princeton tasting is not a fraud case but a comparative tasting event.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: What was the Marquis de Pombal's contribution to wine history, and why is it considered groundbreaking?
options: He invented Port wine

In 1756, he established the Douro as the world's first legally demarcated and regulated wine region, predating France's AOC by nearly 180 years, including rules on permitted grape varieties, vineyard boundaries, and quality control
He was the first to export Madeira to England
He developed the solera system for Sherry aging
explanation: The Marquis de Pombal (Sebastião José de Carvalho e Melo) created the Companhia Geral da Agricultura das Vinhas do Alto Douro in 1756, establishing the Douro as the world's first demarcated wine region. This included boundary markers, production rules, quality grading, and price controls — a remarkably modern regulatory framework that predated all other wine appellation systems.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: A collector presents a bottle of Tokaji Aszú labeled '6 Puttonyos.' They ask about the historical significance of the puttonyos system. What is the most complete answer?
options: Puttonyos is just a marketing term with no historical basis

The puttonyos system dates to the 17th century: a puttony is a traditional hod (basket) holding approximately 25kg of aszú (botrytized) berries added to a 136-liter gönc barrel of base wine. More puttonyos meant more concentrated sweetness. The system historically ranged from 3 to 6 puttonyos, with Eszencia above that. Hungary's 2013 regulations simplified classifications.
It refers to the number of years the wine is aged
Puttonyos indicates the number of grape varieties in the blend
explanation: The puttonyos system is one of the world's oldest wine classification methods for sweet wines. Each puttony of aszú berries added to a gönc barrel increased sweetness and concentration. Tokaji Aszú was the world's first officially classified wine (1730, predating even Bordeaux 1855), and the term 'wine of kings, king of wines' was attributed to Louis XIV regarding Tokaji.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: Recent archaeogenomic studies have revealed what about the domestication history of Vitis vinifera?
options: Grapevines were domesticated only once, in Georgia

Research published in Science (2023) suggests that Vitis vinifera was likely domesticated independently in at least two locations — the South Caucasus and Western Asia — with extensive subsequent admixture, challenging the single-origin hypothesis
All modern wine grapes descend from a single Roman cultivar
Grape domestication occurred in China before any Western evidence
explanation: A major 2023 archaeogenomic study published in Science analyzed thousands of grape samples and suggested that wine grape domestication likely occurred independently in both the South Caucasus (Georgia/Armenia) and a second Western Asian center, with subsequent gene flow between populations. This challenges the long-held single-origin model and reframes our understanding of viticulture's origins.
sources: Science (2023) — Dual domestication origins for grapevines; Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: An MS exam question asks: 'Explain how the 19th-century interplay of oidium (1850s), phylloxera (1860s-1900s), and downy mildew (1878) collectively reshaped European viticulture and wine commerce.' Which answer is most comprehensive?
options: These pests only affected France and had no broader impact

The triple crisis devastated yields, destroyed vineyards, and created massive wine shortages that drove fraud; oidium necessitated sulfur treatments, phylloxera required complete replanting on American rootstocks, and downy mildew required Bordeaux mixture — collectively introducing chemical viticulture, grafting, and ultimately appellation laws to combat fraud born of scarcity
Phylloxera was the only significant crisis; oidium and mildew were minor issues
These events led to the abandonment of European winemaking and the shift to New World production exclusively
explanation: The three crises were sequential hammer blows: oidium (powdery mildew) arrived first in the 1850s, devastating yields and requiring sulfur spraying. Phylloxera followed in the 1860s, ultimately requiring complete replanting on American rootstocks. Downy mildew arrived in 1878, requiring copper sulfate treatments (Bordeaux mixture). Together, they transformed European viticulture from a largely untreated, own-rooted practice to a chemically managed, grafted system, and the resulting wine shortages drove the fraud that led to appellation legislation.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); Wine Science (Jamie Goode)

question: What is a Georgian qvevri?
options: A type of oak barrel
A large egg-shaped clay vessel buried underground, used for fermenting and aging wine — a tradition dating back approximately 8,000 years
A stone wine press
A type of Georgian grape variety
explanation: Qvevri (also spelled kvevri) are large clay vessels, often holding 800+ liters, lined with beeswax and buried underground. Grapes are fermented with skins, seeds, and sometimes stems inside the qvevri. This UNESCO-recognized tradition produces the amber (orange) wines Georgia is famous for.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage listing for qvevri winemaking

question: What was the significance of the Abbé Rozier's 'Cours Complet d'Agriculture' (1781-1800) for viticulture?
options: It was a cookbook with no viticultural content
It was one of the first systematic encyclopedic works to classify grape varieties by their characteristics and recommend specific varieties for specific terroirs, prefiguring modern ampelography
It was purely about cereal farming
It only described winemaking in Bordeaux
explanation: The Abbé François Rozier's encyclopedic work included detailed ampelographic descriptions and was among the first to systematically match grape varieties to terroir. His work laid groundwork for Pierre Viala and Victor Vermorel's later definitive Ampélographie (1901-1910), which classified thousands of varieties.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); World Atlas of Wine (Johnson & Robinson)

question: Which statement about the history of Champagne is NOT accurate?
options: English scientist Christopher Merret documented méthode champenoise before Dom Pérignon's tenure at Hautvillers
Veuve Clicquot (Nicole-Barbe Ponsardin) developed the riddling table (pupitre) for rémuage around 1816
The comet vintage of 1811 was celebrated as one of Champagne's greatest early vintages
Dom Pérignon personally invented the cork closure for Champagne bottles in 1688
explanation: Cork closures predated Dom Pérignon and were in use for wine bottles in England before his time. While Pérignon contributed to Champagne quality (blending, pressing), the attribution of cork invention to him is a myth. Merret's 1662 paper, Clicquot's riddling innovation (~1816), and the famous 1811 comet vintage are all historically documented facts.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: The 'Vinland' references in Norse sagas (c. 1000 CE) have been debated for over a century. What is the current scholarly consensus on whether Norse explorers encountered wild grapevines in North America?
options: The sagas are entirely fictional and have no historical basis

Archaeological evidence at L'Anse aux Meadows (Newfoundland) confirms Norse presence in North America c. 1000 CE, but 'Vinland' may refer to wild grapes (Vitis riparia) encountered further south, or the name may derive from 'vin' meaning meadow/pasture in Old Norse rather than wine — the debate remains unresolved among scholars
Norse explorers definitely made wine from American grapes
Vinland was located in modern-day California
explanation: While L'Anse aux Meadows confirms Norse exploration of North America, the Vinland-grape connection remains debated. Wild Vitis species (V. riparia, V. labrusca) did grow in regions south of Newfoundland. Some scholars argue 'vin' in Old Norse could mean 'meadow' rather than 'wine.' Butternuts found at L'Anse aux Meadows suggest travel to areas where wild grapes also grew, but definitive proof of Norse winemaking in America is lacking.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); Smithsonian Institution archaeological reports on L'Anse aux Meadows

question: What was the impact of the 18th Amendment (US Prohibition, 1920-1933) on American wine grape acreage?
options: All vineyards were destroyed

Paradoxically, vineyard acreage in California actually increased during Prohibition because demand for home winemaking grapes surged, though quality declined as thick-skinned shipping varieties like Alicante Bouschet replaced fine varieties
Vineyard acreage remained exactly the same
Only European imports were affected; domestic production was unchanged
explanation: Counterintuitively, California grape acreage expanded during Prohibition to meet demand for home winemaking (legally permitted up to 200 gallons/household). However, fine varieties were ripped out in favor of thick-skinned grapes like Alicante Bouschet that could survive cross-country rail shipment. This quality decline took decades to reverse after Repeal.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: What is the generally recommended serving temperature for full-bodied red wines?
options: Ice cold (4-6°C / 39-43°F)
Room temperature, which in wine terms means 16-18°C (60-65°F)
Frozen
explanation: Full-bodied reds are best served at 16-18°C (60-65°F), which is 'room temperature' from the era of European cellars — not modern heated rooms. Serving too warm accentuates alcohol and makes the wine flabby; too cold mutes aromas and amplifies tannins.
sources: WSET Level 2 Study Guide; The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: In professional wine service, who at the table should taste the wine first?
options: The oldest person at the table
The person who ordered the wine (the host)
Everyone tastes simultaneously
The server tastes first
explanation: The host (the person who ordered the wine) is presented the bottle for confirmation, then poured a small taste to check for faults. Once the host approves, the wine is served to guests (traditionally women first, then men, host last).
sources: Court of Master Sommeliers Service Standards; WSET Level 3 Study Guide

question: Which is NOT a recognized wine fault?
options: Cork taint (TCA)
Brettanomyces (barnyard aromas)
Oxidation (sherry-like notes in a young white)
High acidity in a Riesling from Mosel
explanation: High acidity is a natural and desirable characteristic of Mosel Riesling, not a fault. Cork taint (TCA contamination), Brettanomyces (producing 4-ethylphenol and 4-ethylguaiacol), and premature oxidation are all recognized wine faults that may warrant returning a bottle.
sources: WSET Level 2 Study Guide; Wine Science (Jamie Goode)

question: What are the three ideal conditions for long-term wine storage?
options: Warm, bright, and humid
Cool (12-14°C / 53-57°F), dark, and at 60-75% humidity with minimal vibration
Cold (0°C / 32°F), dry, and well-ventilated
Room temperature (22°C / 72°F), any lighting, and low humidity
explanation: Ideal wine storage requires consistent cool temperature (12-14°C), darkness (UV light degrades wine), and moderate humidity (60-75% to prevent cork desiccation without encouraging mold). Vibration should be minimized as it can disturb sediment and potentially accelerate chemical reactions.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); WSET Level 3 Study Guide

question: A guest sends back a bottle of wine claiming it is 'corked.' You smell the wine and detect wet cardboard and muted fruit. What is the correct course of action?
options: Argue with the guest that the wine is fine
Agree the wine shows TCA taint, apologize, remove the bottle, and offer a replacement bottle or alternative selection without charging for the faulted bottle
Pour the wine for other tables to finish
Add ice to mask the fault
explanation: TCA (2,4,6-trichloroanisole) contamination produces musty, wet cardboard aromas and suppresses fruit. A corked bottle should always be replaced without charge. The sommelier should confirm the fault, validate the guest's concern, and offer a replacement. The faulty bottle should be set aside for distributor credit.
sources: Court of Master Sommeliers Service Standards; Wine Science (Jamie Goode)

question: Match each wine style with its recommended serving temperature range.
explanation: Sparkling wines are served coldest (6-8°C) to maintain mousse and freshness. Light whites slightly warmer (7-10°C). Full-bodied whites need warmth (10-13°C) to reveal complexity. Full-bodied reds at 16-18°C to balance fruit, tannin, and alcohol expression.
sources: WSET Level 3 Study Guide; Court of Master Sommeliers Service Standards

question: When should you decant a wine?
options: Only red wines over 20 years old
Young tannic reds benefit from aeration to soften tannins and open aromas; old wines with sediment should be carefully decanted to separate clear wine from sediment
All wines should always be decanted
Only white wines benefit from decanting
explanation: Decanting serves two purposes: for young wines, it provides aeration to help volatile compounds develop and tannins soften. For old wines, it separates clear wine from sediment that has formed during aging. Note that very old, fragile wines should be decanted gently and served promptly, as excessive oxygen exposure can cause them to fade rapidly.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); Court of Master Sommeliers Service Standards

question: What is the standard restaurant wine markup, and what is the 'sweet spot' price point strategy?
options: Restaurants mark up all wines 5x wholesale cost

Typical markups range from 2.5-4x wholesale; savvy wine directors often use progressive pricing — lower percentage markup on expensive bottles — to encourage guests to trade up, creating a 'sweet spot' where quality-to-price value peaks
All wines are sold at cost plus a flat $10 fee
Markups are regulated by law and must be exactly 3x
explanation: Restaurant wine markups typically range from 2.5-4x wholesale cost, with many programs using progressive pricing: higher percentage markups on entry-level bottles and lower percentages on premium bottles. This creates a value 'sweet spot' in the mid-range where guests get better quality-to-price ratio, which also builds guest trust and loyalty.
sources: GuildSomm Business of Wine; Court of Master Sommeliers Business Module

question: A guest orders a 2005 Barolo. You notice significant sediment in the bottle. What is the proper service approach?
options: Shake the bottle vigorously to redistribute the sediment before pouring

Stand the bottle upright for as long as possible, then carefully decant using a candle or light source to separate wine from sediment, presenting the decanted wine and showing the guest the original bottle
Refuse to serve it and suggest a younger wine instead
Pour directly from the bottle without concern for sediment
explanation: For an aged wine with sediment: ideally stand it upright before service (24+ hours preferred, but work with available time), then decant carefully using a light source (traditionally a candle) to illuminate the neck and stop pouring when sediment reaches the shoulder. Present both the decanter and the original bottle so the guest can see the label.
sources: Court of Master Sommeliers Service Standards; Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: Which is NOT a standard element found on an Old World (European) wine label?
options: Appellation of origin (e.g., Appellation Bordeaux Contrôlée)
Vintage year
Grape variety prominently displayed on the front label
Producer/estate name
explanation: Traditional Old World labels emphasize place (appellation) and producer rather than grape variety. In contrast, New World labels (Australia, USA, Chile) typically feature the grape variety prominently. There are exceptions (Alsace labels grape variety; some New World wines use proprietary names), but this is the general convention.
sources: WSET Level 3 Study Guide; The Wine Bible (Karen MacNeil)

question: What is the ISO 3591 standard wine tasting glass, and why is it significant?
options: It is a specific brand of crystal glass

The ISO 3591 glass is an internationally standardized tulip-shaped glass designed to provide consistent sensory evaluation conditions — with specific dimensions (height, bowl diameter, rim diameter) to concentrate aromas while allowing fair comparison across wines
It is the largest wine glass available
ISO 3591 refers to the minimum alcohol content for wine
explanation: The ISO 3591:1977 tasting glass has standardized dimensions: approximately 155mm tall, 65mm bowl diameter, 46mm rim diameter, holding ~215ml. Its inward-tapering rim concentrates volatile aromas at the opening. It is used in professional tasting contexts to eliminate glassware as a variable, ensuring all wines are evaluated under identical conditions.
sources: Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz); ISO 3591:1977 specification

question: Match each wine fault with its primary chemical cause.

right: 4-ethylphenol and 4-ethylguaiacol
2,4,6-trichloroanisole (TCA)
Acetic acid and ethyl acetate
Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) and mercaptans

explanation: TCA causes cork taint. Acetic acid (with ethyl acetate at higher levels) causes volatile acidity. H2S and mercaptans cause reductive faults. Brettanomyces yeast produces 4-ethylphenol (4-EP) and 4-ethylguaiacol (4-EG), responsible for barnyard, band-aid, and medicinal off-flavors.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: A sommelier notices that a guest is ordering an expensive bottle solely to impress their dining companions, but the wine won't pair well with their food choices. What is the ethically correct approach?
options: Say nothing and open the expensive bottle — the guest is always right

Gently suggest the wine's pairing limitations and offer an alternative that suits both the food and the occasion, while emphasizing the alternative's quality — upselling to the right bottle, not just the expensive one
Refuse to open the expensive bottle
Publicly point out the poor pairing choice in front of the guest's companions
explanation: Sommelier ethics require balancing guest satisfaction with honest guidance. The best approach is tactful: acknowledge the guest's choice, mention the pairing challenge diplomatically, and suggest an alternative that suits the food while still being impressive for the occasion. This builds trust and demonstrates the sommelier's value without embarrassing the guest.
sources: Court of Master Sommeliers Ethics and Service Standards; GuildSomm Business of Wine

question: What is the concept of 'by-the-glass program management' in a restaurant context?
options: Simply opening any bottle and pouring from it until it is finished

Strategic selection of BTG offerings considering pour cost, preservation technology (Coravin, nitrogen systems), rotation frequency, food menu compatibility, and price-point spread to maximize both guest satisfaction and profitability while minimizing waste
Serving only house wine by the glass
Only offering sparkling wine by the glass
explanation: A well-managed BTG program balances multiple factors: pour cost targets (typically 20-25% of retail), wine preservation technology to extend shelf life, rotation frequency to maintain freshness and create discovery opportunities, variety across styles and price points, and food menu compatibility. Modern systems like Coravin and nitrogen dispensing have transformed BTG possibilities for premium wines.
sources: GuildSomm Business of Wine; Court of Master Sommeliers Business Module

question: Which factor is LEAST important when designing a restaurant wine list?
options: Food menu compatibility and pairing potential
Diversity of regions, styles, and price points
The sommelier's personal preference for only natural wines
Inventory turnover rate and cash flow management
explanation: A sommelier's personal bias should not dictate list composition. Professional wine list design prioritizes food compatibility, guest preferences, diverse representation (regions, styles, prices), and business metrics (turnover, margins, cash flow). While a sommelier's expertise shapes the list, personal ideology should not override guest needs and business reality.
sources: GuildSomm Business of Wine; Court of Master Sommeliers Business Module

question: How does the shape of a wine glass affect aromatic perception?
options: Glass shape has no measurable effect on wine perception

Bowl size and shape affect the surface area of wine exposed to air (influencing oxidation and volatile release), while the rim diameter and chimney shape direct the concentration and angle of aromas toward different areas of the nose, altering which volatile compounds are perceived most prominently
Only the color of the glass matters
Wider glasses always make wine taste better
explanation: Research by Riedel and subsequent academic studies have shown that glass geometry affects wine perception through multiple mechanisms: bowl volume affects head space and surface area; chimney shape concentrates or disperses volatiles; rim diameter controls how broadly aromas reach the nose. While the magnitude of the effect is debated, measurable differences in sensory perception have been demonstrated.
sources: Wine Science (Jamie Goode); Oxford Companion to Wine (Robinson, Harding & Vouillamoz)

question: A restaurant owner asks you to build a 200-selection wine list for a new upscale Italian restaurant. What organizational structure would you recommend?
options: Alphabetical by producer name

Organize by Italian region (north to south), with sub-sections by style (sparkling, white, rosé, red), include a curated non-Italian section representing key international benchmarks, and structure pricing progressively with clear BTG selections that preview the list's strengths
Random order to encourage exploration
Only by price from cheapest to most expensive
explanation: For an Italian restaurant, regional organization (north to south mirrors Italy's geography and is intuitive) with style sub-categories provides logical navigation.
